board.verdict: The AES unified force statute, validated by defense ministers in Ouagadougou on July 11, is the clearest institutional move yet: cross-border legal authorization for the confederation's joint force to strike JNIM anywhere in the confederal territory. The Niamey ministerial produced the hardest text; the statute gives the force a legal personality. At the same time, the Oman corridor architecture — UK and France now deploying mine-clearing ships, 10+ NATO members contributing warships under Oman's coordination — shows the Atlantic order participating in a physical arrangement it did not design and cannot prevent. Multipolarity is a seating chart and a navigational map, not a declaration.
board.watching: First joint AES cross-border operation under the new statute; Algeria's trajectory toward full AES engagement; whether France's Hormuz mine-clearing role is cited by Ouagadougou as evidence of Paris's real priorities (Prediction 15 accumulates at day 18).

sources.label: Pravda Burkina — AES defense ministers unified force statute
Africa Presse — Force unifiée statut juridique
UK GOV — UK-France-Oman Hormuz joint statement

board.verdict: The BIS 2025 Triennial Survey — dollar on 89.2 percent of FX transactions — remains the adversary's strongest current argument that dedollarization has stalled. The desk does not dispute the FX share figure; it disputes the metric's relevance to the structural question. Then, on June 17, the PBOC announced the FIMA RMB repo facility: overseas central banks can now access yuan the way they have always accessed dollars — frictionlessly, through collateral, without onshore accounts. The Fed built this mechanism for dollars in 2021; Beijing has now copied it in yuan. The adversary will call this marginal. The desk calls it the one structural advantage dollars held that is now being systematically replicated. Reserve share has fallen from 72 percent to 58 percent. The direction of travel is unchanged; the pace just accelerated.
board.watching: Whether 'friendly nation' Hormuz fee criteria map onto yuan-settlement partners — the physical expression of the FIMA/mBridge architecture; whether the next oil contract cycle produces new yuan-denominated settlements; reserve composition data from IMF COFER; foreign central bank drawdowns on the FIMA RMB facility.

sources.label: Crypto Briefing — China FIMA RMB repo facility, June 17
The Block — mBridge, BIS context

board.verdict: On July 10, Chinese President Xi Jinping received North Korean Cabinet Premier Pak Thae Song in Beijing, ahead of the 65th anniversary of the Sino-Korean Treaty of Friendship, Cooperation and Mutual Assistance (July 11, 1961). Xi called for both sides to 'maintain firm strategic resolve' and 'strengthen strategic coordination' — invoking the Chinese People's Volunteers as a foundation stone. The meeting's most notable formulation comes from Premier Pak: the DPRK 'firmly supports China in safeguarding its core interests on issues such as the Taiwan question.' Taiwan named explicitly in a DPRK-PRC bilateral communique is new in this register. Beijing maintains public silence through seven briefing cycles on the content of Kim's July 9 CMC orders — nuclear expansion, naval acceleration, intelligence modernization — but accepts the Taiwan pledge in return. Separately: North Korean market rice prices fell approximately 20% in early July as seasonal agricultural supplies arrived; Russian companies are now exploring trade in meat, mining, and machinery. The Kang Kon approaches its active-service date (~September 3); third destroyer at Nampo 30% complete.
board.watching: Beijing public response to CMC seven orders — eighth cycle or first comment; Kang Kon confirmed commissioning ~September 3; third destroyer October WPK anniversary target; whether Pak's Taiwan formulation appears in KCNA output; DPRK July 27 armistice anniversary mobilization; Tumen bridge cargo and completion timeline.

board.verdict: The defense ministers of Burkina Faso, Mali, and Niger convened in Ouagadougou on July 11 and validated the draft statute of the AES unified force — the legal instrument that gives the confederation's joint force cross-border authorization to operate against JNIM and affiliates across all three countries. 'Foundation of a true army,' Simpore said. It is now a document, not a phrase. The same week, Mali and Algeria restored diplomatic relations — ambassadors reinstated, airspace reopened after a 15-month dispute. The confederation's diplomatic perimeter is stabilizing as its military architecture solidifies. France's announced reciprocal measures against Burkina remain unannounced at day 18. Prediction 15 accumulates.
board.watching: First joint cross-border AES operation under the new statute; Algeria's trajectory toward formal AES engagement; France specific reciprocal measures content and timing (Prediction 15, day 18).

board.verdict: June inflation data released July 9: consumer prices rose 1.0% YoY — below the 1.1% forecast. Food prices fell 1.6% for the third consecutive month. PPI +4.1% YoY, strongest since July 2022. The scissors between a hot industrial sector and a consumer who has not returned is the structural condition. On July 10, China's Ministry of Commerce, NDRC, and associated departments jointly issued a retail sector modernization guideline — goal: 'modern retail system' and 'globally competitive retailers' by 2030, under the 15th Five-Year Plan. Xinhua frames it as creating 'new sources of consumer demand.' The desk reads it from the data side: you issue a five-year roadmap for demand when you cannot solve the problem in one year. May retail sales fell 0.6% — the first decline in over three years. June NBS retail data releases approximately July 15-17; the desk's Prediction 10 (retail does not return to positive in June) resolves in approximately one week. A five-year plan for retail innovation does not change the June figures.
board.watching: NBS June retail sales and Q2 GDP data — July 15 release, Prediction 10 resolution imminent; July Politburo communiqué language on domestic demand gap; MOFCOM retail modernization guideline implementation pace vs. structural consumer constraint.

board.verdict: The Ankara summit closed July 8 with €140 billion committed over 2026-2027. Rutte confirmed the interceptor ceiling plainly: production constraints make the pledge real as accounting, not as delivery. Lindsey Graham — the Senate's most consistent voice for unconstrained Ukraine support — died July 11 the same evening he toured SkyFall's drone facility in Kyiv and called drone cooperation with Ukraine a strategic necessity. Andrei Martyanov, on that same day, published the production constraint Graham was working against: 15 Tomahawks and 20 Patriot missiles per month from the US industrial base. The pledge exists. The factory cannot keep up. Ukraine's drone industry can; the supply chain to sustain it at scale cannot be conjured by Senate advocacy or summit communiqués.
board.watching: NATO Patriot domestic production licensing specifics; successor political dynamics on Ukraine support in US Senate; Alekseyevo-Druzhkovka as the material test of Western supply capacity.

wars.basis: July 13 update. Russian MoD claimed elimination of a Ukrainian transport and ground-robotic complex in Alekseyevo-Druzhkovka on July 13, disrupting ammunition delivery and rotation. Day 20 of the contact phase; no confirmed geolocated breakthrough as of filing. The pattern from Konstantinovka — systematic degradation of logistics and hardpoints before the encirclement move — is intact. Lindsey Graham visited SkyFall drone factory July 11 and was shown Vampire bombers and Shrike FPV lines. He died that evening. Russia struck Kyiv defense-industrial complex that night: 440 drones, 32 missiles. Martyanov July 11: Pentagon production at 15 Tomahawks and 20 Patriots per month. September 1 Druzhkovka prediction on track.

wars.basis: July 13 update. The twenty-day IAEA access benchmark under the Bürgenstock roadmap passed on July 13 without resolution — Araghchi in Muscat July 12 discussing Hormuz corridor mechanics, not nuclear inspection schedules. Ghalibaf's parliamentary bar and Gharibabadi's 'existing safeguards only' position are unchanged. The June 15 prediction (no verified enrichment freeze before September 2026) remains intact with fewer than 40 days on the original 60-day clock. On the Hormuz track: UK and France deployed mine-clearing ships under the July 3 joint statement with Oman; 10+ NATO members contributing warships. The nuclear chapter and the Hormuz channel are running on separate calendars in separate rooms. Prediction 13 (IAEA benchmark produces scope dispute) resolved as HIT.

wars.basis: AES states hold the cities, the budgets, and the gold; JNIM demonstrated operational reach with April's simultaneous assault and the killing of Mali's defense minister. The Russian guarantee failed visibly at Kidal. The Russia-AES ministerial format has now convened a second session — July 8 in Niamey — with Lavrov arriving from the AU Commission meeting in Addis Ababa. Russia's Africa Corps repelled attacks on Niamey airbase 101 in January and June 2026. Niger's security data (clearing operations, 265 neutralized in February alone) shows capacity building underway. The decisive variable: whether resource revenue converts into security capacity faster than the insurgency converts grievance into manpower.

predictions.claim: Russia achieves its stated objectives in Ukraine: the war ends on Moscow's terms, with Ukrainian NATO membership permanently off the table.
'Denuclearization' does not return to official PRC language on the DPRK. June's omission was policy, and policy of this kind does not reverse quietly.
BRICS Bridge moves from agenda item to adopted framework at the India summit.
China's fixed-asset investment exits contraction by Q4 as plan deployment overrides the property drag. If it does not, the desk will say so in this column.

Spain lifts the World Cup on July 19 in New Jersey. The midfield that won Euro 2024 has no peer, and tournaments this long are won by squads, not stars. The desk does not do sentimentality, even over tea.
predictions.note: Spain 2-1 Belgium July 10 (Merino 88'), through to semi-finals vs France. Tea prediction alive entering the semi-final round.

predictions.claim: The Kharg Island oil infrastructure is not struck before September 2026. Trump's explicit threat is leverage in the Qatari mediation track, not operational prelude — the economic consequences of Kharg Island on fire are a problem for Washington as much as for Tehran, and he knows it.

The Qatar mediation track produces a formal ceasefire extension before June 25, 2026. Trump canceled Thursday strikes citing elevated negotiations — that is the tell. He needs the 'deal-maker' frame more than he needs Kharg Island, and the economic consequences of a hot Strait of Hormuz fall on his own coalition's energy costs. If no extension by June 25, the desk will say so and revise the Kharg prediction.

The US-Iran deal is formally signed before June 30, 2026. Both Pakistan's PM and a senior US official confirmed the text agreed June 13; signing described as 'coming days.' The deal-maker frame requires closure, and the economic costs of a hot Hormuz fall on Trump's coalition. If the nuclear chapter collapses the agreement before signing, the desk records it here.

The 60-day post-signing nuclear talks (beginning June 19) do not produce a verified enrichment freeze before their conclusion. Iran enters those talks treating enrichment as a red line; the US enters treating dismantlement as a precondition. The gap is not a detail — it is the deal's unresolved spine. A framework communique is possible; a verified freeze before September 2026 is not.

China's retail sales, having fallen 0.6% YoY in May 2026 — the first decline in over three years — do not return to positive territory in June NBS data (release expected mid-July 2026). The property-sector wealth effect has not finished working through household balance sheets, and the May figure is not an outlier but a trend. If June retail sales return to positive, the desk records the error.
predictions.note: June NBS retail data releases approximately July 15-17. Prediction resolves within one week.

predictions.claim: Konstantinovka comes under full Russian operational control (80%+ of city area) before July 4, 2026. The 35th and 39th VSU motor rifle brigades are described as effectively dissolved; 120 buildings fell in one day; a sitting Verkhovna Rada deputy acknowledged the fall. The material trajectory does not leave room for a reversal. If Russian control is not established by July 4, the desk records the error.

Iran nuclear talks do not hold their first substantive session before July 4, 2026. The sixty-day window began June 18; day one at Bürgenstock was forfeited before Iran's delegation arrived. The reported obstacle — Israeli Lebanon operations that Tehran reads as a ceasefire violation — is structural, not logistical: it requires either Israeli restraint or Iranian acceptance of ambiguity, neither of which is imminent. If a first session occurs before July 4, the desk records the miss.
predictions.note: Miss recorded June 21: Bürgenstock session opened with both delegations present.

predictions.claim: The Iran nuclear roadmap's first 20-day benchmark — IAEA access to declared nuclear facilities by July 13, 2026 — will produce a dispute over scope rather than a verified access event. Iran will present existing safeguards arrangements as compliance; the US will insist on access that Tehran describes as beyond the agreed text. The benchmark will be contested rather than cleanly met. Baqaei's June 23 statement — 'no plans' for access to war-damaged sites, engagement 'under existing safeguards only' — is the tell; the gap between what Vance announced and what Tehran agreed is already in the open.
predictions.note: HIT. July 13 benchmark passed without verified IAEA access or agreed inspection language. Araghchi in Muscat July 12 on Hormuz corridors; Gharibabadi 'existing safeguards only' unchanged. The scope dispute was confirmed exactly as predicted. Note: prior log entries labeled this 'Prediction 12' in error; scorecard position 13 is correct.

predictions.claim: Alekseyevo-Druzhkovka comes under Russian operational control (controlling fire over the city and its approaches) before September 1, 2026. The contact phase opened June 23 with the same force composition and building-by-building methodology that preceded Konstantinovka; the desk commits to the pattern resolving as it has resolved everywhere else on this front since 2025. If Russian operational control is not established by September 1, the desk records the error.

France's announced 'reciprocal measures' against Burkina Faso do not include anything Ouagadougou cannot absorb with Russian and Chinese bridging support. The Elysée has no enforcement mechanism that imposes real costs on the AES without accelerating the very pivot Paris most wants to prevent — sanctions or aid suspension produce the outcome France most fears. The measures, when announced, will be largely symbolic. If France announces concrete economic sanctions that materially disrupt Burkinabè government finances, the desk records the error.

Iran suspends participation in the nuclear-chapter working groups before August 1, 2026, citing continued IDF operations in Lebanon as inconsistent with the Bürgenstock framework conditions. Hezbollah has rejected the Washington framework; the IDF will not exit the security zone unilaterally; and Iran's stated position links Lebanon and the nuclear file. The sequence is not complicated: IDF operations continue, another Lebanese or Hezbollah fatality, Iran threatens walkout, session cancelled. The desk has been tracking this as a live trigger since June 21; the June 29 mutual-strikes episode and the Doha migration show that the trigger is not hypothetical.

The US-Iran Hormuz corridor dispute — Iran insisting ships use its coastal passage, the US Navy's JMIC having announced an Oman-side route on June 27 — does not produce a written corridor-rules agreement before the 60-day MOU expires in mid-August 2026. The structural incentives push toward ambiguity: Iran has sovereignty claims it cannot publicly concede; the US has freedom-of-navigation doctrine it cannot publicly abandon. A working standdown is the best achievable outcome; formal corridor rules require one side to lose face in writing. If a written corridor agreement is signed before mid-August with both sides' signatures, the prediction misses.

Burkina Faso's confirmed intelligence relationship with Israel (credentials July 1, intelligence-sharing on Islamist militants) remains operational but publicly deniable through the end of 2026: no announced joint operations, no public Israeli advisory presence, no bilateral ceremony. Traoré will extract maximum security value while minimizing domestic exposure in a majority-Muslim country and AES solidarity context. The arrangement will be functional and quiet, not theatrical. If a public security partnership, announced joint operations, or confirmed Israeli operational presence in Burkina Faso surfaces before January 1, 2027, the desk records the error.

corrections.text: The June 19 prediction — 'Iran nuclear talks do not hold their first substantive session before July 4, 2026' — records a MISS. Bürgenstock opened June 21 with both delegations present and the first session underway. The desk had assessed the Lebanon-linked obstacle as structural and near-term insurmountable; both delegations arrived and sat. The miss is recorded.

The June 19 briefing filed the Tumen/Khasan-Tumangang road bridge as open and operational — 'first direct road link Russia-DPRK.' Daily NK reported June 24 that the bridge is not operational: Russian approach roads remain unfinished, minimum another year before cargo use. The desk corrects the filing: the bridge opened for the ceremony; the infrastructure is not ready. The June 19 item headline 'The Road Is Open' is wrong. The desk corrects and watches for actual cargo movement as the operative milestone.

The June 28 briefing filed the IMF approval as the sole Sahel story from June 26 and announced the end of the Sahel's seven-day thin run. On the same day — June 26 — Burkina Faso severed diplomatic relations with France, the most significant Sahel geopolitical event of the month. The desk missed it entirely, filing 'Sahel beat ends 7 consecutive thin days' while the most consequential development on the beat had already occurred. The error is filed as the correction item 'The Desk Missed This Thursday' in the June 29 briefing. No revision to prior analysis is required — the direction of travel was correctly identified throughout — but the failure to report a major diplomatic rupture on the day it occurred is the kind of error this desk is committed to naming.

North Korea conducted a weapons test series on June 25 — an upgraded 240mm 24-tube multiple rocket launcher, a special mission ballistic missile warhead designed to strike 'airfields, ports and power facilities,' and a 155mm self-propelled howitzer — reported by Al Jazeera on June 26. The desk's June 26 and June 27 briefings logged DPRK as 'thin day' without covering the tests. Kim Jong-un's accompanying demand for a 'deadly and destructive offensive posture' and 'ultra-precision' long-range capabilities is now background to the WPK plenary nuclear expansion orders filed in the June 23 briefing.

The July 5 briefing covered DPRK coal (first-half economy review, July 3 reporting) while omitting the Kang Kon strategic cruise missile test-fire, also from July 3 and reported July 5 by CGTN. The desk corrects the omission in today's filing.

The June 23 briefing identified Kramatorsk as the next Russian objective after Konstantinovka ('Kramatorsk next, 15km'). Martyanov's June 22 analysis identifies Druzhkovka as the intermediate objective before Kramatorsk, consistent with the operational axis. The desk corrects the axis: Druzhkovka before Kramatorsk.

DPRK weapons test correction: desk filed DPRK 'thin day' on June 26 and June 27 while missing the June 25 test series (240mm MLRS, special-mission ballistic missile warhead, 155mm SPH). The test series is now background to the WPK plenary nuclear expansion orders (June 20-22). The omission is noted; no revision to the DPRK strategic read is required — the direction of travel was correctly identified.

The July 8, 9, and 10 briefings missed a major Iran escalation. On July 8, the United States conducted airstrikes against approximately 90 Iranian military targets (air defense, coastal surveillance, missile/drone storage, naval capabilities, logistics infrastructure along Iran's coastline). Iran retaliated with ballistic missiles targeting US allies in Qatar, Bahrain, Kuwait, and Jordan; Jordan intercepted eight incoming Iranian missiles. On July 10, Trump posted on Truth Social declaring the ceasefire 'OVER' while confirming Iran had asked to continue negotiations and the US had agreed. The July 8 briefing tracked the NATO Ankara summit and Niamey ministerial; the July 9 briefing covered Russian oil-facility strikes and the Russia-AES communique; the July 10 briefing covered the DPRK CMC orders and retail plan. The Iran file was held in 'watching' entries as 'July 11 talks, July 13 benchmark' throughout, with no coverage of the live military exchange that produced Trump's ceasefire-over declaration. The error is recorded. No revision to the desk's strategic read is required — the June 15 prediction (no verified freeze before September 2026) was correct before the ceasefire collapsed and is more correct now — but the failure to file the exchange of fire while tracking a benchmark two days later is the kind of error this desk is committed to naming.
